sections.markdown: # Intro to Grail and DQL - Part 2

## EXERCISE 1: Warm Up 

## Please duplicate the Notebook before editing it. If you need instructions, check out the Lab Guide. Thanks!

##### In Part 1 of this course we learned about about the below commands that we will revisit in this Warmup :  
* `fetch` allows us to indicate the data type want to search
* `fields` limits the fields that are displayed.  `fields` command also allows for implicit use of `fieldsAdd`
* `filter` narrows the returned data set to only data needed for the specific use-case 
* `parse` allows for the slicing of data in strings into dedicated fields
* `fieldsFlatten` will automatically "flatten" nested objects into numerous individual fields 
* `expand` allows to expand an array into separate records

Let's revisit each of these concepts in the next few exercises

##### Exercise 1.1 - `FETCH`

Use the `fetch` and `filter` commands to return `logs` data for :
  * `k8s.deployment.name` equal to `accounting`
  * `service.name` equal to `accounting`

##### Exercise 1.2 - `FIELDS`

Copy/Paste the DQL from the prior exercise to this one  
Use the `fields` command to reduce the columns that are returned :  

* `timestamp,`
* `k8s.pod.name,`
* `service.name,`
* `k8s.node.name,`
* `k8s.namespace.name,`
* `k8s.deployment.name,`
* `@orderresult,`  
* `dt.entity.cloud_application_instance`

HINT: Because teh `@orderresult`  field starts with a special character `@` we must reference the field name differently

##### Exercise 1.3 - `FILTER`

* Copy/Paste the DQL from the prior exercise to this one
* Use the `filter` command to remove log events where `@orderresult` has `null` data

#### Overview for remaining exercises

For the purpose of our eCommerce reporting use-case we want to access the "productId", and "quantity" data.  This data is nested deeply within `@orderresult` field.  The next few exercises we will perform steps to access it :

* If we look closely at field `@orderresult` we can find the term "items".  "items" consists of the transactional data from each transaction.  
* Because these records are preceded by the term "items", this data is known as the `items` array. 

NOTE: "items" often contains multiple "item" records, which indicates that multiple items were purchased in the transaction.   
```
"items": [ 
   { "item": { "productId": "9SIQT8TOJO", "quantity": 4 }, "cost": { "currencyCode": "USD", "units": "3599" } }, 
   { "item": { "productId": "66VCHSJNUP", "quantity": 3 }, "cost": { "currencyCode": "USD", "units": "349", "nanos": 949999999 } } ] }
```

##### Exercise 1.4 - `PARSE`

* Copy/Paste the DQL from the prior exercise to this one
* Use the `parse` command to generate a new field named `json_order_result`, that is sourced from the `@orderresult` string data.  
HINT: In your parse command, use `"JSON:"` to identify the data is formatted as JSON

##### Exercise 1.5 - `FIELDSADD` and `EXPAND`

After Exercise 1.4 we can now see that we have yet another new field named `json_order_result[items]`.  This is the "items" array.

* Copy/Paste the DQL from the prior exercise to this one
* Use `fieldsAdd` to create a new field named `items_json` and set the field equal to the name of our `json_order_result[items]` array
* Use `expand` to "flatten" the data in the `items_json` field

##### Exercise 1.6 - `FIELDSADD` 

We now have `[item]` and `[cost]` data nested under `items_json`.  Lets extract this out further:

We will use `fieldsAdd` to create 4 new fields.

Copy/Paste the DQL from the prior exercise to this one  
Use `fieldsAdd` to create the below fields from the `[item]` array and the `[cost]` array
* product_order=items_json[item]
* product_price=items_json[cost]

Run the above

Next, Use `fieldsAdd` to create the below fields from the `[productId]` and `[quantity]` found in `product_order`
* productId=product_order[productId]
* quantity=product_order[quantity]

##### Exercise 1.7 - `FIELDSREMOVE`

Because we have extracted the data we need, as a final step remove these fields as they are no longer needed

* Copy/Paste the DQL from the prior exercise to this one  
* Use `fieldsRemove` to remove fields: `product_order`, `product_price`, `items_json`, `json_order_result`

#### Conclusion

We will use this finalized DQL for Exercise 2, so copy it and have it at the ready.

Altogether your final DQL should look something like this :

```
fetch logs
| filter k8s.deployment.name=="accounting"
| filter service.name == "accounting"
| fields timestamp,  k8s.deployment.name, k8s.namespace.name, k8s.pod.name, service.name, `@orderresult`, dt.entity.cloud_application_instance
| filter isNotNull(`@orderresult`)
| parse `@orderresult`, "JSON:json_order_result"
| fieldsAdd items_json=json_order_result[items]
| expand items_json
| fieldsAdd product_order=items_json[item], product_price=items_json[cost]
| fieldsAdd productId=product_order[productId], quantity=product_order[quantity]
| fieldsRemove product_order, items_json, json_order_result,product_price
```
